scenarios.name: Specific Performance of Sale Agreement

checks.detail: Specific Relief Act / specific performance cited
At least one statutory section cited
Prayer for execution of sale deed
No invented amounts (10L/50L never in facts)

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE CITY CIVIL COURT
AT BENGALURU

O.S. No. __________ / 20____

Anita Sharma,
Aged about 52 years,
Occupation: {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: Indiranagar, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

Rajesh Reddy,
Aged about 60 years,
Occupation: {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: HSR Layout, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE OF CONTRACT RELATING TO IMMOVABLE PROPERTY WITH INTEREST AND COSTS

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

JURISDICTION

1. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute the present suit. The Defendant is also a person sui juris and can be sued.

2. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction to try and entertain the present suit inasmuch as the cause of action arose within the local limits of the jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court at Bengaluru, and the Defendant carries on business / resides within such territorial limits.

3. This Hon'ble Court has pecuniary jurisdiction to try the present suit, the suit being valued at Rs. {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE_WORDS}} Only), which is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Hon'ble Court.

LIMITATION

4. The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under Article 54 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which provides a period of three years from date fixed for performance, or if no date fixed, when plaintiff has notice that performance is refused. The suit is filed well within the prescribed limitation period.

FACTS OF THE CASE

5. The Plaintiff states that on 10.01.2024, she entered into a written Agreement to Sell with the Defendant regarding the purchase of a residential property being Flat No. 302, 3rd Floor, Sunshine Apartments, Koramangala, Bengaluru, measuring 1200 sq ft. The total sale consideration agreed upon between the parties was Rs. 85,00,000/-. The said Agreement to Sell is marked as Annexure A.

6. Pursuant to the terms of the Agreement to Sell, the Plaintiff paid an advance amount of Rs. 25,00,000/- to the Defendant on 10.01.2024 itself. The payment was made via RTGS bearing UTR No. HDFC20240110987654. The proof of said payment is marked as Annexure B.

7. As per the terms recorded in the Agreement to Sell, the balance sale consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/- was agreed to be paid on or before 10.07.2024 against the execution and registration of the final Sale Deed. The Plaintiff has always been ready and willing to perform her part of the contract including payment of the balance consideration.

8. The property is free from all encumbrances and the Defendant had represented clear title to the same at the time of executing the Agreement to Sell. The Plaintiff has maintained sufficient funds to complete the transaction within the stipulated timeline.

BREACH OF CONTRACT AND REFUSAL

9. Despite the agreement subsisting and the timeline for performance being valid until 10.07.2024, the Defendant vide a letter dated 05.06.2024 communicated his refusal to execute the Sale Deed. The Defendant cited the receipt of a higher offer from a third party as the reason for such refusal. The said refusal letter is marked as Annexure D.

10. Upon receipt of the Defendant's refusal, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated 20.06.2024 calling upon the Defendant to honour the Agreement to Sell and execute the Sale Deed as per terms. The legal notice along with the postal receipt is marked as Annexure C.

11. The Defendant has failed and refused to comply with the legal notice and has not come forward to execute the Sale Deed. The Defendant has thus committed a clear breach of the contract dated 10.01.2024, forcing the Plaintiff to approach this Hon'ble Court for relief.

DAMAGES

12. By reason of the Defendant's breach and refusal to perform, the Plaintiff has suffered significant financial loss. The Plaintiff has been deprived of the use of the advance amount of Rs. 25,00,000/- paid to the Defendant since 10.01.2024. The Plaintiff is entitled to interest on the said amount for the period of detention.

13. The Plaintiff has incurred expenses towards stamp duty, legal notices, and court fees in pursuing this remedy. The Plaintiff is entitled to recover these costs along with the costs of this suit from the Defendant due to the Defendant's wrongful refusal to perform the contract.

LEGAL BASIS

14. The present cause is governed by Specific Relief Act, 1963, including Section 10 (mandatory after 2018 Amendment — court SHALL grant unless S.11(2), S.14, or S.16 exception), Section 16, Section 21, Section 22.

15. The present cause is governed by Indian Contract Act, 1872, including Section 10.

16. The Plaintiff also relies on Transfer of Property Act, 1882, including Section 53A, where the pleaded facts attract those provisions.

CAUSE OF ACTION

17. The cause of action for the present suit first arose on 05.06.2024 when the Defendant committed the breach / wrongful act giving rise to the Plaintiff's right to institute the present suit.

18. The cause of action continues to subsist as the Defendant has failed to perform his obligations under the Agreement to Sell.

19. The cause of action is not a continuing cause of action. The breach consisted of a single event. However, the Plaintiff's right to damages subsists.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

20. The suit is valued at Rs. {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE_WORDS}} Only) for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee. Appropriate court fee has been paid on this plaint under the provisions of the Court Fees Act, 1870 as applicable in the State of Karnataka. The plaint is properly stamped.

INTEREST

21. The Plaintiff claims interest on the suit amount as follows:

    (a) Pendente lite interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and just from the date of filing of this suit till the date of judgment / decree, under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;
    (b) Future interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and just from the date of judgment / decree till the date of realisation of the entire decretal amount.

PRAYER

In the premises aforesaid, the Plaintiff most respectfully prays that this Hon'ble Court be pleased to:

    (a) Pass a decree for specific performance of the Agreement to Sell dated 10.01.2024, directing the Defendant to execute and register the Sale Deed in favour of the Plaintiff upon payment of the balance consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/-;
    (b) In the alternative, if specific performance is not granted, pass a decree in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant for a sum of Rs. {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE_WORDS}} Only) towards damages and refund of advance with interest;
    (c) Award pendente lite and future interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems just under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;
    (d) Award costs of the suit including costs of the legal notice;
    (e) Grant such other and further relief(s) as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

LIST OF DOCUMENTS

Annexure A — Original agreement dated 10.01.2024 for sale of flat

Annexure B — RTGS receipt (UTR: HDFC20240110987654) for advance payment

Annexure C — Legal notice dated 20.06.2024 with postal receipt

Annexure D — Defendant's refusal letter dated 05.06.2024

VERIFICATION

Verified at {{CITY}} on this the ____ day of __________ 20____.

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of paragraphs 1 to 13 of this plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and no material facts have been concealed therefrom, and that I have not filed any other suit, petition, or proceeding in any court or before any authority in respect of the same subject matter and cause of action.

DEPONENT

{{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}

PLAINTIFF

scenarios.name: Breach of Contract — Damages (Business)

checks.detail: Total claim Rs.32L or breakdown stated

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE COMMERCIAL COURT (COMMERCIAL DIVISION)
AT BENGALURU

Commercial Suit No. __________ / 20____

TechSoft Solutions Pvt Ltd,
A company registered under the Companies Act,
Having its registered office at MG Road, Bengaluru,
Represented by its Director Priya Menon, aged 40 years.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

CloudBridge Systems LLP,
A limited liability partnership having its office at Whitefield, Bengaluru,
Represented by its Designated Partner Vikram Singh, aged 48 years.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR DAMAGES FOR BREACH OF CONTRACT WITH INTEREST AND COSTS

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

COMMERCIAL COURT MAINTAINABILITY

1. The subject matter of the present suit arises out of a commercial dispute within the meaning of Section 2(1)(c) of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015.

2. The suit is valued above the specified value as prescribed under Section 12 of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, and is therefore maintainable before this Hon'ble Commercial Court.

JURISDICTION

3. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute the present suit. The Defendant is also a person sui juris and can be sued.

4. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction to try and entertain the present suit inasmuch as the cause of action arose within the local limits of the jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court at Bengaluru, and the Defendant carries on business within such territorial limits.

5. This Hon'ble Court has pecuniary jurisdiction to try the present suit, the suit being valued at Rs. 32,00,000/- (Rupees Thirty-Two Lakhs Only), which is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Hon'ble Court. This Hon'ble Court has subject matter jurisdiction to entertain commercial disputes under the Commercial Courts Act, 2015.

LIMITATION

6. The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under Article 55 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which provides a period of three years from when the contract is broken. The suit is filed well within the prescribed limitation period.

SECTION 12A COMPLIANCE

7. {{MEDIATION_STATUS -- CONFIRM: (a) Was pre-institution mediation conducted under Section 12A of Commercial Courts Act? If yes, provide mediation authority, date, and outcome certificate. (b) If not, state whether urgent interim relief is being sought. Section 12A compliance is mandatory for commercial suits unless urgent interim relief is sought.}}

FACTS OF THE CASE

8. On 01.02.2024, the Plaintiff and Defendant entered into a Software Development Agreement for the development of an ERP system. The total contract value was fixed at Rs. 45,00,000/-. A copy of the said Agreement is annexed hereto as Annexure A.

9. Pursuant to the terms of the Agreement, the Plaintiff paid the first milestone payment of Rs. 20,00,000/- to the Defendant on 01.02.2024 via NEFT. The transaction bears UTR Number ICIC20240201456789. Proof of payment is annexed hereto as Annexure B.

10. As per the agreed timeline, the Defendant was obligated to deliver the first module of the ERP system on or before 01.05.2024.

11. The Plaintiff issued a legal notice to the Defendant dated 01.07.2024 demanding performance and refund. A copy of the said legal notice is annexed hereto as Annexure E.

BREACH PARTICULARS

12. The Defendant failed to deliver the first module by the agreed deadline of 01.05.2024, thereby committing a breach of the terms of the Agreement.

13. On 15.06.2024, the Defendant expressly abandoned the project and ceased all development work. This abandonment was communicated via email correspondence, copies of which are annexed hereto as Annexure C.

14. Despite the abandonment and the subsequent legal notice, the Defendant has failed to refund the advance payment or perform obligations under the Agreement.

DAMAGES

15. Due to the breach and abandonment, the Plaintiff is entitled to the refund of the advance payment of Rs. 20,00,000/- paid to the Defendant.

16. The Plaintiff has suffered consequential losses amounting to Rs. 12,00,000/- due to delay in operations caused by the non-delivery of the ERP system. The assessment of loss is supported by an auditor's report, annexed hereto as Annexure D.

17. The Plaintiff claims total damages of Rs. 32,00,000/- (Rs. 20,00,000/- refund + Rs. 12,00,000/- consequential damages) along with interest at the rate of 18% per annum from the date of breach until realization.

LEGAL BASIS

18. The Defendant has committed a repudiatory breach by refusing to perform its obligations, entitling the Plaintiff to treat the contract as at an end and seek appropriate relief.

19. The Plaintiff is entitled to compensation under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, which provides that when a contract has been broken, the party who suffers by such breach is entitled to receive compensation for any loss or damage caused thereby, which naturally arose in the usual course of things from such breach, or which the parties knew, when they made the contract, to be likely to result from the breach of it.

CAUSE OF ACTION

20. The cause of action for the present suit first arose on 15.06.2024 when the Defendant abandoned the project and committed the breach giving rise to the Plaintiff's right to institute the present suit.

21. The cause of action is continuing in nature as the Defendant continues to retain the advance payment without performance or refund.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

22. The suit is valued at Rs. 32,00,000/- (Rupees Thirty-Two Lakhs Only) for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee. Appropriate court fee of {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} has been paid on this plaint under the provisions of the Court Fees Act, 1870 as applicable in the State of Karnataka. The plaint is properly stamped.

INTEREST

23. The Plaintiff claims interest on the suit amount as follows:

    (a) Interest at 18% per annum from the date of breach (15.06.2024) until realization;
    (b) Pendente lite interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and just from the date of filing of this suit till the date of judgment/decree, under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;
    (c) Future interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and just from the date of judgment/decree till the date of realization of the entire decretal amount.

PRAYER

In the premises aforesaid, the Plaintiff most respectfully prays that this Hon'ble Court be pleased to:

    (a) Pass a decree in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant for a sum of Rs. 32,00,000/- (Rupees Thirty-Two Lakhs Only) towards damages;
    (b) Award Rs. 20,00,000/- towards refund of advance payment;
    (c) Award Rs. 12,00,000/- towards consequential loss/damages;
    (d) Award interest at 18% per annum from 15.06.2024 until realization;
    (e) Award pendente lite and future interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems just under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;
    (f) Award costs of the suit including costs of the legal notice;
    (g) Grant such other and further relief(s) as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

SCHEDULE OF DAMAGES

The Plaintiff claims the following heads of damages:

24. Refund of Advance Payment
    Amount claimed: Rs. 20,00,000/-
    Basis: First milestone payment made on 01.02.2024, no performance received
    Documentary proof: Annexure B (NEFT receipt)

25. Consequential Loss/Damages
    Amount claimed: Rs. 12,00,000/-
    Basis: Delay in operations caused by non-delivery of ERP system
    Documentary proof: Annexure D (Loss assessment report by auditor)

TOTAL DAMAGES CLAIMED: Rs. 32,00,000/-

LIST OF DOCUMENTS

Annexure A — Software Development Agreement dated 01.02.2024

Annexure B — NEFT receipt (UTR: ICIC20240201456789)

Annexure C — Email correspondence showing abandonment

Annexure D — Loss assessment report by auditor

Annexure E — Legal n
